copy.title: Spurs force Game 7. Gerard leads Schwab. Market quietly climbing.
copy.sportsAngle:
# SPORTS

Gregg Popovich just forced a Game 7 in San Antonio. The Spurs dismantled OKC 118-91 last night behind Tim Duncan's 28 points and suffocating defense that made SGA look mortal. Winner advances to the Finals.

**Conversation line:** "Duncan had more points than OKC's entire bench. That's how you close out a series at home."
copy.marketsTake:
**Markets**

Microsoft's 3.5% rip yesterday had nothing to do with the broader market—it was pure Copilot momentum after their AI integration announcements, while the Magnificent Seven stayed mostly flat despite the headline gains. Tech leadership is narrowing to specific names executing on AI, which means next week's earnings will separate the actual revenue drivers from the story stocks.

Watch for whether that Microsoft move sticks or reverses when reality checks in. If MSFT holds above $440, you're looking at a genuine rerating. If it doesn't, the AI trade gets crowded in the wrong way real fast.
copy.golfNote: Ryan Gerard's -6 lead at Schwab is holding up because his iron game is elite right now.
copy.sharpTake:
# SHARP TAKE

The Spurs forced Game 7 in Oklahoma City. Tim Duncan is 39 and still closing games—the Thunder got outrebounded by a team that shouldn't have any left. SPY is up on a quiet weekend, which means Monday will tell you whether this rally has legs or if we're just treading water into earnings season. Saturday's setup: Game 7 is live, Ryan Gerard has one hand on the Schwab trophy at -6, and the market is waiting for someone to break the tie.

Clear Saturday afternoon for Game 7. If someone asks what you're doing, the answer is no.
